title: Welcome to Unreal: Launcher, Editor & Your First Movement Toy
html:
This is your path

You finished Foundations, and you chose Unreal Engine 5 as your single engine. Good choice. This track stands entirely on its own: you will not need any other engine to build a portfolio. By the end of these nine lessons you will have shipped a connected arc of small games spanning many genres, all in Unreal: a movement toy, a 3D collectathon, an FPS shooting gallery, a physics push/obstacle course, a time-trial racer, a wave shooter, a physics puzzle room, and finally a polished, packaged capstone level you can show employers.

Each lesson teaches concepts the engine actually needs, then ends with a Build This game that either extends what you built before or kicks off a brand-new genre. You will write both Blueprints (visual scripting) and C++, and learn when each is the right tool.

The Epic Games Launcher

Everything starts at the Epic Games Launcher. Install it, sign in, and open the Unreal Engine tab. Here you install engine versions, browse Fab (the asset marketplace), and manage projects. Install UE 5.4 or newer. Engine installs are large (tens of GB) because they include source, templates, and starter content.

Creating a project

Click Launch, then pick a category (Games), then a template. Templates are starting points:

  • Blank — nothing but the essentials.
  • Third Person — a character you can run around with, camera behind. Perfect for us this lesson.
  • First Person — a gun and an FPS camera.

Choose Blueprint as the project default (you can add C++ anytime). Enable Starter Content for free meshes and materials. Pick a folder, name it, and create.

The editor at a glance

The Unreal Editor is a set of dockable panels:

PanelWhat it does
ViewportThe 3D world. Navigate with right-mouse + WASD.
OutlinerA tree of every Actor in the level.
DetailsProperties of whatever is selected.
Content BrowserAll your assets (meshes, materials, Blueprints).
ToolbarSave, Play (PIE), Settings, Build.
Play In Editor.
The big Play button (PIE) runs the game inside the editor instantly — no compiling, no packaging. Esc stops it. This is your fastest iteration loop; use it constantly.

Navigation muscle memory

  • Right-mouse held + WASD — fly around like a camera.
  • Alt + left-drag — orbit a point.
  • F — focus the selected Actor.
  • W / E / R — Move / Rotate / Scale gizmos.

Saving and project structure

Your project is a folder on disk. The important parts: Content/ holds all assets, Config/ holds .ini settings, and a .uproject file launches it. Save the level often (Ctrl+S) and Save All from File before closing — Unreal does not autosave aggressively.

Lab.
Create a Third Person Blueprint project with Starter Content. Press Play and run the default mannequin around. Practice viewport navigation until flying feels natural. Drag a few Starter Content props (a chair, a cube) from the Content Browser into the level, then move/rotate/scale them with W/E/R.
🎮 Build This: Movement Toy — Stroll

Genre: third-person movement prototype. This STARTS our arc — it is the first game and the foundation everything else extends.

  1. Create the Third Person template project (Blueprint, Starter Content).
  2. Delete the default floor and build a small playground: drop several Starter Content cube and ramp meshes to make platforms at different heights.
  3. Scale a large cube flat to act as ground so the character cannot fall off the world.
  4. Press Play and verify you can run, jump, and navigate every platform.
  5. Save the level as L_Stroll. You now have a playable toy and a clean base to grow.

title: Projects, Levels & the Content Browser
html:
What is a level?

A Level (also called a Map, file extension .umap) is a container of Actors — everything you place in the world: meshes, lights, cameras, volumes, and your gameplay objects. A project can hold many levels: a main menu, several gameplay maps, a test sandbox. The level your game opens with is set in Project Settings > Maps & Modes > Editor Startup Map / Game Default Map.

The Content Browser is your library

The Content Browser shows everything inside Content/. Treat it like a real codebase — organize from day one or you will drown later.

Folder hygiene.
Make top-level folders: Blueprints/, Maps/, Materials/, Meshes/, UI/, Audio/. Prefix asset names by type: BP_ Blueprint, M_ material, MI_ material instance, SM_ static mesh, L_ level, WBP_ UI widget. Future-you will thank present-you.

Importing and finding assets

Drag files (FBX, PNG, WAV) straight into the Content Browser to import them. Use the search bar and the filter dropdown to find assets by type. Right-click an asset for actions like Duplicate, Reference Viewer (shows what uses it), and Migrate (copy an asset and all its dependencies into another project).

Actors and the Outliner

Every object in a level is an Actor. The Outliner lists them. You can group Actors into folders in the Outliner (purely organizational) and attach Actors to each other to form parent/child hierarchies (moving the parent moves the children).

Transform: the core of placement

Every Actor has a Transform in the Details panel: Location, Rotation, Scale. Unreal units are centimeters — a value of 100 is one meter. Mobility matters too:

  • Static — never moves; cheapest, can bake lighting.
  • Movable — can move at runtime; needed for anything gameplay drives.

World Settings and project settings

World Settings (a panel) configures the current level — most importantly its GameMode Override (more in Lesson 4). Project Settings configures the whole project: input, rendering, default maps, packaging. Get comfortable opening both.

project-layout.txt
MyGame/ Content/ Blueprints/ BP_* Maps/ L_* Materials/ M_* MI_* Meshes/ SM_* UI/ WBP_* Config/ DefaultEngine.ini ... MyGame.uproject
Lab.
In your Stroll project, create the folder structure above. Move the level into Maps/ and rename it L_Stroll. Create a second empty level (File > New Level > Empty) named L_Sandbox for experiments. Set L_Stroll as the Editor Startup Map.
🎮 Build This: Movement Toy — Stroll: The Playground

Genre: still the movement prototype. This EXTENDS Stroll from Lesson 1 — we are turning a flat test into an organized, deliberate playground level.

  1. Organize your project into the folder structure from the Lab.
  2. Block out a proper obstacle playground using Starter Content meshes: a starting platform, a series of jumps with gaps, and a tall tower of platforms to climb.
  3. Group related Actors into Outliner folders (Floor, Jumps, Tower).
  4. Set every non-moving mesh to Static mobility.
  5. Add a goal platform at the top with a Starter Content light above it. Save. You now have a clean, navigable course ready for gameplay.

title: Blueprints AND C++: When to Use Each
html:
Two languages, one engine

Unreal gives you two ways to write gameplay logic. They are not rivals — professionals use both, together.

  • Blueprints — a visual node graph. You wire events to actions. Fast to iterate, no compile step, designer-friendly, great for prototyping and high-level glue.
  • C++ — Unreal's native language. Faster at runtime, version-control friendly (plain text), and the only way to touch some low-level systems. Requires a compiler and a build step.

When to use which

Use Blueprints whenUse C++ when
Prototyping fastPerformance-critical loops
Designer-tweakable valuesCore systems / base classes
Level-specific one-off logicReusable, testable code
UI wiringHeavy math, large data

The common professional pattern: write a C++ base class that holds the heavy logic and exposes tunable values, then create a Blueprint child of it to set those values and add level-specific tweaks. Best of both worlds.

Your first Blueprint

Right-click in the Content Browser > Blueprint Class > pick a parent (e.g. Actor). Open it: the Event Graph is where you wire logic. Key nodes:

  • Event BeginPlay — fires once when the Actor spawns.
  • Event Tick — fires every frame (use sparingly; it costs CPU).
  • Print String — prints debug text to the screen.

Drag from a node's output pin to create the next node; the white wire is execution flow, colored wires are data.

Variables & functions.
In the left My Blueprint panel, add Variables (typed data) and Functions (reusable graphs). Check a variable's eye icon to make it editable from the Details panel — this is how you expose tunables to designers.

Your first C++ class

From the editor: Tools > New C++ Class. Pick a parent (Actor), name it, and Unreal generates a header (.h) and source (.cpp) and adds a compiler. UPROPERTY and UFUNCTION macros expose C++ to Blueprints and the editor.

Spinner.h
UCLASS() class AMySpinner : public AActor { GENERATED_BODY() public: UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere, Category="Spin") float SpinSpeed = 90.f; virtual void Tick(float Dt) override; };
Spinner.cpp
void AMySpinner::Tick(float Dt) { AddActorLocalRotation(FRotator(0, SpinSpeed*Dt, 0)); }

Compile (the editor's Live Coding, Ctrl+Alt+F11, hot-reloads most changes). EditAnywhere makes SpinSpeed show up in the Details panel of any instance — a designer can tune it without touching code.

Lab.
Make a Blueprint Actor that on BeginPlay prints "Hello from Blueprint". Then make a C++ Actor class with an EditAnywhere float and a Tick that rotates it. Place both in L_Sandbox and watch them work.
🎮 Build This: Collectathon — Coin Rush (foundations)

Genre: 3D collectathon — a NEW genre (pickups + score). This STARTS a new game built on the playground level you made in Lesson 2.

  1. Create a C++ Actor class ACollectible with an EditAnywhere float SpinSpeed and a Tick that rotates it (so coins visibly spin).
  2. Create a Blueprint child BP_Coin of ACollectible; give it a small spinning Starter Content mesh and set SpinSpeed in the Details panel.
  3. Scatter ten BP_Coin instances across your playground platforms.
  4. For now, on BeginPlay each coin prints its name to screen (proof of life). We will make collecting them work next lesson with components and collision.

title: Actors, Components & the Gameplay Framework
html:
Actors are built from Components

An Actor is a thing in the world, but it does almost nothing by itself — its capabilities come from Components attached to it:

  • Static Mesh Component — renders a 3D model.
  • Collision components (Box, Sphere, Capsule) — detect overlaps and block movement.
  • Audio, Light, Particle, Movement components — add behavior.

One component is the Root; everything else attaches under it. In a Blueprint, the Components panel (top-left) is where you add and arrange them.

Collision & overlap events

To make a pickup, add a collision shape and respond to overlap. A collision component fires OnComponentBeginOverlap when something enters it. You filter what can overlap via Collision Presets (e.g. OverlapAllDynamic).

Overlap vs Block.
Overlap = things pass through but you get an event (pickups, triggers). Block = physical collision that stops movement (walls, floors). Set this per channel in Collision Presets.

The Gameplay Framework

Unreal ships a structured set of base classes. Learn these four — they appear in every game:

ClassResponsibility
GameModeThe rules: score, win/lose, which Pawn/Controller to use. Server-only.
Pawn / CharacterThe physical body the player controls. Character adds walking/jumping.
PlayerControllerThe player's intent: input, possessing a Pawn, owning the HUD.
GameState / PlayerStateReplicated game-wide and per-player data.

The flow: the GameMode spawns a PlayerController, which possesses a Pawn. Input goes Controller to Pawn. The GameMode decides who wins.

Wiring a custom GameMode

Create a Blueprint GameMode (or C++ child of AGameModeBase), then in World Settings > GameMode Override, assign it and set its Default Pawn Class. Now your level uses your rules.

Enhanced Input (the modern input system)

UE5 uses Enhanced Input: you define Input Actions (e.g. IA_Jump, a boolean) and bind them in an Input Mapping Context (e.g. Spacebar to IA_Jump). The PlayerController adds the context, the Pawn reacts to the actions. The Third Person template already wires this — study its BP_ThirdPersonCharacter graph.

Lab.
Open BP_Coin. Add a Sphere Collision component as root, mesh under it. Set the sphere to OverlapAllDynamic. Add an OnComponentBeginOverlap event; check if the other actor is the player character; if so, Destroy Actor. Play and walk into a coin — it vanishes.
🎮 Build This: Collectathon — Coin Rush

Genre: 3D collectathon. This EXTENDS Coin Rush from Lesson 3 into a real, scored game.

  1. Give BP_Coin a sphere collision that overlaps the player and destroys itself on pickup.
  2. Create BP_CoinGameMode with an integer variable Score. On pickup, the coin calls a custom event on the GameMode to add one to Score and print the total.
  3. Create BP_CoinController (a PlayerController) and assign it plus your Third Person Character in the GameMode.
  4. Set the GameMode Override in World Settings. Collect all ten coins; when Score reaches 10, print "You Win!".
  5. Save. You now have a complete collect-and-score loop driven by the gameplay framework.

title: Materials, the Material Graph & an FPS Shooting Gallery
html:
What a material is

A Material describes how a surface looks and reacts to light. Unreal uses a physically based rendering (PBR) model, so materials are defined by physical-ish inputs that feed the Main Material node:

InputMeaning
Base ColorThe surface's albedo (its flat color).
Metallic0 = non-metal, 1 = metal.
Roughness0 = mirror, 1 = matte.
NormalFake surface detail via a normal map.
EmissiveLight the surface emits (glow).

The Material Editor graph

Double-click a material to open its node graph (it looks like a Blueprint). You wire nodes into the Main node's inputs. Common nodes: Constant (a number), Constant3Vector (an RGB color), Texture Sample (an image), Multiply / Add / Lerp (math), and Time + Sine (animation).

Parameters & Material Instances

If you convert a Constant into a Parameter (right-click > Convert to Parameter), you can create a Material Instance (right-click the material > Create Material Instance) and tweak that parameter without recompiling the graph. This is hugely efficient — one master material, many cheap instances in different colors.

One master, many instances.
Build a master material with color/roughness/emissive parameters, then make instances MI_Red, MI_Gold, MI_GlowBlue. Changing the master updates them all; changing an instance is instant and free.
emissive-pulse.graph
Time --> Sine --> *0.5 +0.5 (0..1 pulse) Pulse * Color --> Emissive Color Color = Constant3Vector parameter

Applying materials

Drag a material from the Content Browser onto a mesh in the viewport, or assign it in the mesh's Details under Materials. Meshes can have multiple material slots (one per surface section).

Dynamic materials at runtime

To change a material from gameplay (e.g. flash a target when hit), create a Dynamic Material Instance in Blueprint (Create Dynamic Material Instance), then Set Vector/Scalar Parameter Value on it. This lets a hit target flash red for a moment.

Lab.
Build a master material with a Color parameter and an Emissive pulse. Make three instances in different colors. Apply them to props in L_Sandbox.
🎮 Build This: FPS Shooting Gallery — Range Day

Genre: FPS shooting gallery — a NEW genre. This STARTS a new game (a fresh First Person level), bringing forward your component, framework, and material skills.

  1. Create a new level L_Range using a First Person Pawn (template character or a Pawn with a Camera + arrow for aim).
  2. Make BP_Target: a mesh + box collision, using a glowing material instance you built this lesson.
  3. On fire input, do a Line Trace from the camera forward; if it hits a BP_Target, swap its dynamic material to a "hit" color, add to score, and destroy it after a short delay.
  4. Create BP_RangeGameMode tracking Score and a 30-second timer. Spawn targets at random points.
  5. Print the final score when the timer ends. Save. You now have a timed shooting gallery.

title: Physics: Rigid Bodies, Forces & a Push Course
html:
The physics engine

Unreal's Chaos physics simulates rigid bodies: gravity, collisions, friction, and forces. To make an Actor physical, enable Simulate Physics on its primitive component and make sure its mobility is Movable.

Key physics properties

PropertyEffect
Simulate PhysicsTurns on the rigid-body sim for the component.
MassHow heavy it is (affects forces).
Linear/Angular DampingHow quickly motion/spin bleeds off.
Enable GravityWhether it falls.
Collision PresetWhat it blocks/overlaps; physics needs Block on relevant channels.

Collision must be set up right

Physics bodies need collision geometry. Simple shapes (box/sphere/capsule) are cheap and stable; complex per-poly collision is expensive. In a Static Mesh's editor you can auto-generate simple collision primitives. For physics to push the player, the player's capsule must Block the physics object's channel and vice versa.

Applying forces from Blueprint

Once a component simulates physics, you can drive it:

  • Add Impulse — an instant kick (like a hit).
  • Add Force — a continuous push (called each tick).
  • Add Radial Impulse — an explosion radiating from a point.
  • Set Physics Linear Velocity — directly set speed.
launch-pad.bp
OnComponentBeginOverlap (other = physics box) Other.GetRootComponent -> AddImpulse( Up * LaunchStrength ) LaunchStrength = 1500 (toggle velocity-change)
Constraints & triggers.
Use Physics Constraint Actors to make hinges, doors, and swings. Use Trigger volumes (a box that overlaps) to fire events — perfect for checkpoints, fall-detectors, and launch pads.

Physics materials

A Physical Material sets friction and restitution (bounciness). Assign one to a surface to make ice slippery or rubber bouncy. Combine with launch pads and ramps for a fun obstacle feel.

Lab.
In L_Sandbox, drop several cubes, enable Simulate Physics on them, and stack them. Make a Blueprint that on overlap applies a radial impulse to nearby physics actors — a one-button explosion. Watch the stack scatter.
🎮 Build This: Physics Push/Obstacle Course — Tumble Run

Genre: physics push / obstacle course — a NEW genre. This STARTS a new game, reusing your Third Person Character from the early lessons as the runner.

  1. Create L_Tumble: a long course with gaps, ramps, and a goal at the end.
  2. Add stacks of physics cubes the player must shove through, and rolling physics balls on ramps that knock the player back.
  3. Build BP_LaunchPad: a trigger that adds an upward impulse to whatever overlaps (player capsule or physics box).
  4. Add a BP_FallVolume at the bottom: if the player overlaps it, teleport them to the last checkpoint trigger they passed.
  5. Place a goal trigger that prints "Course Complete!" and the elapsed time. Save. A chaotic, physics-driven obstacle run.

title: Level Design, Lighting & Lumen — Build a Time-Trial Racer
html:
From blockout to playable space

Good levels start as greyboxing: rough geometry that nails layout and scale before any art. Unreal's Geometry Tools and basic meshes let you block out a track or arena fast. Iterate on the feel first; pretty comes later.

Snapping & measuring

  • Grid snap (top-right of viewport) keeps placement clean — set it to 50 or 100 units.
  • Rotation snap for clean angles.
  • Remember 100 units = 1 meter; a doorway is ~220 units tall.

Lighting basics

Lights are Actors. The core types:

LightUse
DirectionalThe sun — one direction, lights everything.
PointA bulb radiating in all directions.
SpotA cone (flashlight, lamp).
Sky LightAmbient light from the sky/environment.

Lumen — UE5's dynamic global illumination

Lumen is Unreal 5's real-time global illumination (GI) and reflection system. GI means light bounces: a red wall lit by the sun casts a subtle red glow onto the floor, with no baking. Lumen does this dynamically — move a light or open a door and the bounced lighting updates live. This is a huge deal: older engines required slow lightmap bakes for bounced light; Lumen makes lighting fully interactive.

Lumen quick facts.
It is enabled by default in new UE5 projects (Project Settings > Rendering > Global Illumination = Lumen, Reflections = Lumen). It pairs with Nanite (virtualized geometry) and needs reasonably modern hardware. For your portfolio it gives near-cinematic lighting with almost no setup — just place lights and an emissive material and it bounces realistically.

Post-process & mood

Add a Post Process Volume to control exposure, bloom, color grading, and more. Set it to Unbound to affect the whole level. Small tweaks to exposure and bloom dramatically change mood.

Composition tips

  • Guide the eye with light — bright = "go here".
  • Vary height and width so spaces read clearly.
  • Use landmark shapes so players orient themselves.
Lab.
Greybox a closed-loop track with grid snapping. Add a Directional Light + Sky Light, drop a Post Process Volume (Unbound), and place an emissive-material strip along the track edge — watch Lumen bounce its glow onto the road.
🎮 Build This: Time-Trial Racer — Circuit Sprint

Genre: racing / time-trial — a NEW genre. This STARTS a new game, applying your physics (Lesson 6) to a driveable vehicle and your new level-design and Lumen skills to a track.

  1. Greybox a looping track in L_Circuit with start/finish and corners; light it with a Directional + Sky Light and a Post Process Volume.
  2. Make BP_Racer: a physics box (or simple vehicle) the player steers with input — forward applies force, left/right applies steering torque.
  3. Place checkpoint triggers around the loop that must be hit in order; a start/finish trigger completes a lap.
  4. Add BP_RaceGameMode with a running lap timer; record and print the best lap.
  5. Line the track edges with emissive material instances so Lumen lights the asphalt. Save. A lit, timed circuit racer.

title: UI with UMG — HUDs, Menus & a Wave Shooter
html:
UMG: Unreal's UI system

UMG (Unreal Motion Graphics) builds user interfaces from Widget Blueprints (prefix WBP_). Create one via right-click > User Interface > Widget Blueprint. It opens to two tabs:

  • Designer — drag-and-drop visual layout.
  • Graph — the Blueprint logic behind the widget.

Core widgets

WidgetUse
TextLabels, scores, timers.
ButtonClickable; fires OnClicked.
ImageIcons, backgrounds, health bars.
Progress BarHealth, ammo, charge.
Canvas / Vertical Box / Horizontal BoxLayout containers.

Layout: anchors matter

On a Canvas Panel, each widget has an anchor that pins it relative to the screen (top-left, center, stretch). Anchor a health bar to the bottom-left so it stays put on any resolution. Use Box containers for lists that auto-arrange.

Showing a widget on screen

From any Blueprint (often the PlayerController or HUD), Create Widget (pick the class), then Add to Viewport. To remove it, Remove from Parent. For menus, also Set Input Mode UI Only and Show Mouse Cursor; for gameplay HUD use Game Only.

show-hud.bp
Event BeginPlay (PlayerController) CreateWidget(WBP_HUD) -> AddToViewport store ref so we can update it later

Binding data to the UI

Two ways to keep a Text/ProgressBar current:

  1. Property Binding — bind a Text's content to a function that returns the value (easy, runs each frame).
  2. Event-driven updates — call Set Text only when the value changes (more efficient). Prefer this for things that change rarely.
Menus & pause.
A main menu is just a widget shown in a menu level with buttons that Open Level or Quit Game. A pause menu sets Game Paused true, shows a widget in UI-only input mode, and unpauses on resume.
Lab.
Build WBP_HUD with a score Text (top-right) and a health Progress Bar (bottom-left). Add it to the viewport on BeginPlay. Update the score via an event when it changes; bind the health bar's Percent to the player's health.
🎮 Build This: Wave Shooter — Last Stand

Genre: twin-stick / wave shooter — a NEW genre. This STARTS a new game, reusing your shooting logic from the Range (Lesson 5) and dressing it with a full UMG HUD.

  1. Create L_LastStand: an arena with the player Pawn able to move and shoot (line trace or projectile).
  2. Make BP_Enemy: a Pawn that moves toward the player; on contact it deals damage; when shot it dies and adds score.
  3. Build BP_WaveGameMode that spawns escalating waves of enemies with short breaks between them.
  4. Create WBP_HUD showing score, wave number, and a health bar — all bound to live data.
  5. On death, show WBP_GameOver with the final score and a Restart button that reopens the level. Save. A complete arcade survival loop with real UI.

title: Packaging & Capstone: Ship a Polished, Portfolio-Ready Game
html:
One more genre: a physics puzzle room

Before we package, let's prove how far Blueprint logic alone can carry a game by sketching a physics puzzle room. Puzzles are pure logic: triggers, state variables, and physics constraints. A pressure plate is a trigger that flips a boolean; a door is a Blueprint that opens when all required booleans are true; a movable physics crate is something the player shoves onto the plate. Wire these together and you have emergent puzzles with no new engine systems — just the Actors, components, physics, and Blueprint logic you already know.

From editor to a real, shippable build

So far you have played everything in PIE. Packaging cooks your content and compiles a standalone executable a stranger can run without Unreal installed. This is what turns a project into a portfolio piece.

Pre-flight project settings

  • Maps & Modes — set Game Default Map to your menu level and a sensible Editor Startup Map.
  • Project Description — game name, company, version, and icon/thumbnail.
  • Supported Platforms / Target Hardware — pick Desktop + your quality target.
  • Packaging — choose Shipping build configuration for a final release (smaller, faster, no debug overhead).

Packaging steps

  1. Platforms > Windows (or Mac) > Package Project from the toolbar.
  2. Choose an output folder. Unreal cooks content (converts assets to platform format) and builds the executable.
  3. When done, open the folder and run the .exe (or .app) — your game, standalone.
Watch out.
First packages are slow (cooking everything). If it fails, read the Output Log — common culprits: a missing default map, a referenced asset that fails to cook, or no platform SDK installed. Fix the first error and repeat; errors cascade.

Polish that makes a portfolio piece

  • Main menu — Play / Quit, game title, clean layout (UMG).
  • Pause menu — Resume / Restart / Quit to menu.
  • HUD — score, timer, health, objective.
  • Audio — footsteps, pickups, music, UI clicks.
  • Lighting — let Lumen and a Post Process Volume set the mood.
  • Win/lose states — clear feedback and a way to replay.
Show your work.
Record a short gameplay clip, write a one-paragraph description (genre, what you built, what tech you used: Blueprints + C++, Lumen, physics, UMG), and keep the packaged build downloadable. That trio is your portfolio entry.
Lab.
Set your Game Default Map to a menu level, choose a Shipping configuration, set the project name and icon, and package a Windows (or Mac) build. Run the standalone executable and confirm the menu, gameplay, and quit all work outside the editor.
🎮 Build This: CAPSTONE — A Polished Playable Level

Genre: your choice — including the new physics puzzle room sketched above. This is the CAPSTONE: it ASSEMBLES the skills and assets from every prior game (movement, collectibles, shooting, physics, racing, waves, puzzle logic, UI) into one finished, packaged title.

  1. Pick one genre you enjoyed most and build a single polished level around it — e.g. a physics puzzle room with crates and pressure-plate doors, a collectathon-platformer with a timed goal, or a wave-shooter arena.
  2. Add a main menu level (WBP_MainMenu: Play, Quit) and set it as the Game Default Map.
  3. Add a UMG HUD (score/health/timer) and a pause menu (Resume/Restart/Quit).
  4. Light it with Lumen + a Post Process Volume; add sound effects and music; add clear win/lose screens with replay.
  5. Mix C++ and Blueprints: at least one C++ base class (e.g. your collectible or enemy) with Blueprint children for tuning.
  6. Package a Shipping build, run the standalone executable end to end, and record a clip. You now have a finished, portfolio-ready Unreal game — and a whole arc of genre prototypes behind it.
